We will experiment with strategies for selecting the target and varying the semantic relatedness of source images, from different augmentations of the same image to temporally adjacent frames.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, more challenging self-supervised pre-training paradigm that explicitly models cross-image dependencies. We expect it to produce features that transfer more effectively to detection and segmentation, particularly on tasks requiring relational reasoning. The method will also provide a controlled study on the value of multi-image context versus single-image reconstruction in representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While MAE reconstructs within a single image, our method compels the model to solve a correspondence problem by piecing together one image from fragments of others. This cross-image reconstruction task is distinct from and complementary to existing contrastive or single-image generative objectives.

proposals.title: Scaling Vision Foundation Models: A Framework for Model-Parallel Training of Billion-Parameter Detectors
proposals.motivation: The success of large-scale vision models like Segment Anything and MAE demonstrates the power of scaling, but current detection frameworks (e.g., Mask R-CNN, ViTDet) are primarily optimized for data-parallel training on single-node hardware. To train truly massive, unified models for open-world detection and segmentation—potentially exceeding tens of billions of parameters—we must develop robust, scalable model-parallel training strategies that can efficiently distribute a single model across hundreds of GPUs, a critical capability not yet fully realized in pre-2026 vision research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting and co-designing advanced model-parallel techniques (e.g., pipeline, tensor, and sequence parallelism) with plain Vision Transformer backbones and task-specific detection heads, we can train single, coherent models at an unprecedented scale (10B+ parameters) that achieve superior performance on open-vocabulary detection and segmentation benchmarks compared to ensembles of smaller models or merely scaled-up data-parallel training.
proposals.method: We will extend the plain ViTDet framework to support hybrid model-parallelism. Specifically, we will implement tensor parallelism within ViT self-attention and MLP blocks using Megatron-LM principles, and pipeline parallelism across transformer layers. We will design a model-parallel, parameter-efficient detection head that operates on sharded feature maps. The training will leverage a massive dataset (e.g., a curated mix of SA-1B, COCO, and LVIS) using a supervised detection loss, with careful analysis of gradient synchronization and activation memory across nodes. We will benchmark scaling efficiency and downstream task performance against strong data-parallel baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, production-ready framework for model-parallel training of billion-parameter vision detection models. It will establish new state-of-the-art results on challenging open-vocabulary detection and segmentation tasks by scaling a single model, and deliver a detailed analysis of the scaling laws, communication bottlenecks, and optimization strategies specific to dense prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While model-parallelism is established in NLP, its application to dense, spatially-aware vision tasks like detection is non-trivial and under-explored. The novelty lies in the co-design of the parallelization strategy with the hierarchical feature processing required for detection, moving beyond simply applying NLP-style parallelism to a ViT backbone.

proposals.title: Generative Feature Inversion: Reconstructing and Editing Images from Dense Detection Features
proposals.motivation: Modern object detectors and segmentation models, like those in the Mask R-CNN and Segment Anything lineage, learn powerful, structured feature representations that encode object semantics, boundaries, and context. However, these features are used almost exclusively for discriminative tasks. Inverting these features to reconstruct images poses a significant, under-explored challenge that could unlock new capabilities for model interpretability, dataset understanding, and conditional image editing based on high-level vision tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dense, spatially-aligned feature maps from a pre-trained, supervised detection model (e.g., a ViTDet backbone) contain sufficient information to train a conditional diffusion model to generate coherent images, and that this process can be controlled to edit images by manipulating specific feature channels associated with object classes or boundaries.
proposals.method: We will use a pre-trained, frozen ViTDet or Mask R-CNN model as a feature extractor. For a large dataset like COCO, we will extract multi-scale feature maps from the FPN. A conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) will be trained to reconstruct the original input image given these feature maps as conditioning. The architecture will be based on a U-Net with cross-attention layers to ingest the multi-scale features. We will experiment with feature disentanglement techniques, such as channel masking based on Grad-CAM, to enable controlled image editing by selectively altering feature inputs before generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for 'seeing through the eyes' of a detector, providing a powerful diagnostic tool for understanding learned representations. It will contribute a new method for semantic image editing driven by task-specific features (e.g., 'remove the feature for *person* and regenerate the scene'). The project will also explore the generative potential of supervised, non-generative visual models.
proposals.novelty: While feature inversion exists for classifiers and GANs, inverting the dense, multi-scale features of a modern *detection* system for high-fidelity, controllable image generation is novel. The approach uniquely bridges supervised discriminative learning and generative modeling by treating detection features as a rich, structured conditioning signal for a diffusion model.

proposals.title: SAM-Lite: Training a Segment Anything Model with Weak and Noisy Supervision
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) demonstrated the power of a foundation model for segmentation, but its training required an enormous dataset of 1.1 billion high-quality masks, a costly and impractical resource to replicate or scale. Meanwhile, vast datasets with weaker annotations (e.g., image-level tags, bounding boxes, noisy pseudo-masks) are abundant. A method to effectively learn precise segmentation from such imperfect signals would dramatically reduce the annotation barrier and enable the creation of more accessible and specialized segmentation foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision transformer backbone, pre-trained with a masked autoencoding (MAE) objective, can be effectively adapted to learn high-quality segmentation through a multi-task, consistency-driven training framework that leverages diverse weak and noisy supervisory signals, approaching the performance of models trained on curated mask data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework building on the plain ViT detector (ViTDet) architecture. First, we initialize the model with an MAE-pretrained ViT backbone, leveraging its strong visual representations. Second, we train the model on a mixture of weakly annotated data (image tags, boxes) and a small seed set of high-quality masks. We will design a unified loss function that includes: (1) a boundary-aware segmentation loss for mask data, (2) a multiple-instance learning loss for image-level tags, (3) a box projection loss for bounding boxes, and (4) a consistency regularization term that enforces agreement between predictions from different augmentations of the same image to mitigate noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a recipe and a model (SAM-Lite) for training segmentation foundation models with drastically reduced reliance on expensive mask annotations. It will provide new insights into how different weak signals contribute to learning precise boundaries. The resulting model will serve as a more accessible baseline for future research and applications in data-scarce domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses weak supervision for specific segmentation tasks, applying it to train a promptable, general-purpose segmentation foundation model like SAM is novel. The key innovation is the multi-signal training framework designed explicitly for a ViT-based architecture pre-trained with a self-supervised objective (MAE), moving beyond standard fine-tuning paradigms.

proposals.title: Semantic-First Segmentation: Decoupling Category Learning from Mask Refinement
proposals.motivation: Foundation segmentation models like Segment Anything (SAM) excel at generating high-quality masks for any prompt but can struggle with accurate semantic categorization, often requiring external classifiers. This decoupling is inefficient for real-world vision systems where recognizing *what* an object is, not just its precise boundary, is paramount. A model that prioritizes semantic understanding could improve downstream performance in tasks like open-vocabulary detection and interactive segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly designing a training objective and architecture that prioritizes semantic feature learning over mask boundary fidelity, we can build a segmentation model with superior recognition and generalization capabilities, even if its initial mask outputs are coarser.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage vision transformer architecture. The first stage uses a masked autoencoder (MAE)-pretrained ViT backbone with a contrastive loss on pooled mask features to enforce strong semantic clustering in feature space, directly informed by category labels. The second, lighter-weight stage refines masks using a modified SAM-style prompt decoder, but trained with a loss that weights semantic correctness higher than boundary IoU. We will ablate the balance between these losses on datasets like COCO and LVIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new model and training paradigm for semantic segmentation that shifts the priority from mask quality to recognition. It will yield insights into the trade-offs between localization and categorization in foundation models. The resulting features are expected to be more effective for few-shot transfer and open-vocabulary object detection.
proposals.novelty: While existing work refines masks for quality or efficiency, we explicitly subordinate mask refinement to semantic learning within a single model framework. This inverts the common paradigm of adding semantics to a segmentation model, instead building segmentation upon a semantically robust foundation.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Promptable Segmentation via Multi-Modal Feature Alignment and Synthetic Prompt Generation
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) demonstrated the power of promptable segmentation but required a massive, billion-mask dataset (SA-1B) for training, which is prohibitive for most researchers and limits adaptation to specialized domains. Achieving robust, interactive segmentation with orders of magnitude less data remains a critical open challenge in computer vision, essential for democratizing the technology and enabling applications beyond web-scale imagery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmentation model can achieve SAM-like promptable generality with drastically less supervised data by strategically leveraging (1) strong, self-supervised pre-trained visual representations (e.g., from MAE) and (2) a training regime that uses synthetically generated prompts to maximize the diversity of segmentation tasks seen during fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will build upon a plain Vision Transformer backbone pre-trained with Masked Autoencoding (MAE) for robust visual features. The model will be fine-tuned for promptable segmentation using a curated mix of high-quality existing datasets (e.g., COCO, LVIS). Crucially, we will design a Synthetic Prompt Engine that, for each training image, generates a diverse set of input prompts (points, boxes, coarse masks) and corresponding target output masks by algorithmically mining the available ground truth. This engine will simulate the long-tail of segmentation tasks, teaching the model to respond to varied prompts. We will explore explicit feature alignment losses between image embeddings and prompt embeddings to improve prompt-conditioning efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available segmentation model with promptable capabilities rivaling SAM but trained on <5% of its data. It will provide a rigorous analysis of the roles of pre-training, prompt diversity, and data composition in achieving data-efficient segmentation. The proposed synthetic prompt generation framework will serve as a blueprint for data amplification in interactive vision tasks.
proposals.novelty: While SAM showed scale is sufficient, we investigate whether architectural and algorithmic priors—specifically, the combination of MAE pre-pretraining and systematic, in-domain synthetic prompt generation—can be sufficient, making promptable segmentation accessible without a billion masks. This shifts the focus from dataset scale to training strategy and representation quality.

proposals.title: SAM-GPT: Grounding Language Models in Segment Anything for Open-World Visual Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) provides a powerful, promptable foundation for image segmentation, but lacks semantic understanding. Conversely, large language models (LLMs) possess rich world knowledge and reasoning capabilities but are not inherently grounded in visual perception. Bridging these modalities is critical for building interactive AI systems that can understand and act upon the visual world through natural language, a core challenge in multimodal AI pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tight, bidirectional integration of SAM's segmentation engine with a frozen, instruction-tuned LLM will enable zero-shot, open-world visual reasoning tasks—such as referring expression segmentation, visual question answering about object attributes, and language-guided image editing—by allowing the LLM to reason over and direct SAM's spatial outputs.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture where a frozen LLM (e.g., Vicuna, LLaMA) acts as a reasoning engine. Visual features from an image encoder are fused with SAM's mask decoder outputs via a lightweight, trainable perceptual adapter. This adapter converts SAM's masks and their associated visual features into a sequence of spatial tokens that the LLM can attend to. The model is trained end-to-end on a curated dataset of image-instruction-mask triplets, where the LLM learns to generate textual responses and, crucially, spatial prompts (e.g., point coordinates, box parameters) that are fed back into SAM's prompt encoder to execute segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, unified framework for vision-language interaction that leverages the strengths of both foundation models without full fine-tuning. It will establish benchmarks for open-world, segmentation-grounded visual reasoning and release a model capable of complex, iterative dialogue about image content with precise spatial grounding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work connects vision encoders to LLMs, the direct, bidirectional coupling of an LLM with a promptable segmentation model like SAM to enable the LLM to *control* a segmentation engine is novel. This moves beyond using SAM as a mere feature extractor to treating it as an actionable tool within a language-driven reasoning loop.

proposals.title: SAM-Lite: A Real-Time Interactive Segmentation Model via Architectural Distillation and Task-Specialized Pruning
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful foundation for interactive segmentation but is computationally heavy, hindering real-time responsiveness crucial for applications like video editing and medical annotation. Pre-2026, efficient vision models often sacrifice versatility for speed, creating a gap for a model that retains SAM's interactive quality while achieving sub-100ms latency on standard hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a combination of architectural distillation from the heavy image encoder into a plain, optimized ViT detector backbone and task-aware pruning of the prompt encoder and mask decoder, we can create a specialized model that matches SAM's interactive accuracy with significantly faster inference.
proposals.method: We will first distill knowledge from SAM's ViT-H image encoder into a pre-trained, plain ViT-Tiny backbone, similar to those explored for detection, using masked feature matching and a boundary-aware loss. Second, we will apply iterative magnitude pruning, guided by the gradient flow from interactive prompts (points, boxes), to sparsify the prompt encoder and mask decoder. Finally, we will optimize the inference pipeline by pre-computing and caching image embeddings where possible and implementing efficient cross-attention kernels for prompt integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, real-time variant of SAM, enabling new interactive applications. It will contribute a methodology for task-specialized architectural compression of foundational vision models, moving beyond general-purpose efficiency toward optimizing for specific user interaction patterns.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior model compression work, our approach explicitly tailors the pruning and distillation process to the unique, multi-stage architecture of an interactive segmentation model, optimizing the entire prompt-to-mask pathway rather than just the vision backbone.

proposals.title: RAG-Seg: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Zero-Shot and Fine-Grained Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Foundation models like Segment Anything (SAM) demonstrate impressive zero-shot segmentation capabilities, but their predictions can be inconsistent and lack semantic grounding, especially for fine-grained or ambiguous objects. Concurrently, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has proven highly effective in NLP for grounding language models in external knowledge. This project aims to bridge these concepts, hypothesizing that retrieving and conditioning on relevant visual exemplars can significantly improve segmentation robustness and semantic accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: Conditioning a segmentation model's predictions on retrieved exemplar masks from a large-scale database will improve zero-shot generalization, semantic consistency, and fine-grained boundary accuracy compared to a monolithic, non-retrieval-based foundation model.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage system: 1) A retrieval module that, given an input image and a query (point, box, or text), uses a joint image-text-mask embedding space (e.g., from a MAE-pretrained ViT) to fetch the k most similar (image, mask) pairs from a database like SA-1B. 2) A generation module, built upon a plain ViT detector/segmenter backbone (e.g., Mask R-CNN with ViT), which is adapted to take the input image and the retrieved masks as explicit context tokens. The model will be trained via supervised learning on segmentation datasets (e.g., COCO, LVIS) with an auxiliary retrieval loss, teaching it to attend to and reason from the provided exemplars.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel retrieval-augmented paradigm for segmentation, moving beyond a single, fixed model. It will deliver a new model, RAG-Seg, with quantifiable improvements in zero-shot and fine-grained segmentation tasks. The proposed architecture and training methodology will provide a blueprint for making large-scale segmentation models more data-efficient and semantically controllable.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is established in NLP, its application to dense pixel-level prediction tasks like segmentation is largely unexplored. The key novelty is framing segmentation as a conditional generation process guided by retrieved visual exemplars, rather than relying solely on the internal parameters of a massive foundation model.

proposals.title: SAM-NAS: Neural Architecture Search for Efficient and Specialized Segment Anything Models
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful foundation model for segmentation, but its monolithic architecture is computationally expensive and not optimized for specific downstream tasks or efficiency constraints. Pre-2026, neural architecture search (NAS) has proven highly effective for discovering optimal architectures in vision tasks like detection and classification, yet it has not been systematically applied to the core components of promptable segmentation models. This gap presents a key opportunity to automate the design of more efficient and performant SAM variants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a task-aware neural architecture search, guided by both foundational segmentation quality and task-specific efficiency metrics, can discover encoder and decoder architectures that significantly outperform the hand-designed SAM components in terms of accuracy-efficiency trade-offs for targeted applications.
proposals.method: We will formulate a joint search space encompassing the Vision Transformer (ViT) based image encoder and the lightweight mask decoder. For the encoder, the search space will include patch embedding strategies, transformer block configurations (e.g., number of heads, MLP ratio, attention types), and staged depth/width scaling. For the prompt-aware decoder, we will search over cross-attention mechanisms and convolution block designs. We will employ a differentiable NAS (DNAS) strategy, such as DARTS or its variants, using a bi-level optimization objective that combines the original SAM's generalized loss on the SA-1B dataset with a task-specific loss (e.g., for interactive segmentation or medical imaging) and a FLOPs/latency regularization term.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A family of SAM-derived architectures (SAM-NAS) Pareto-optimal for different computational budgets and application domains. 2) A NAS framework tailored for large-scale, promptable vision foundation models. 3) Empirical analysis revealing which architectural components most impact SAM's performance and efficiency, guiding future design principles.
proposals.novelty: While NAS is well-studied for classification and detection, applying it to the core components of a billion-scale promptable segmentation foundation model is novel. The key innovation is the joint, efficiency-aware search across the encoder-decoder boundary of a model designed for open-world generalization, moving beyond static, one-size-fits-all architectures.

proposals.title: Revisiting the SVM Frontier: Decoupled Training for Robust Object Detection Heads
proposals.motivation: The transition from SVM classifiers to end-to-end softmax-based networks in object detection (e.g., R-CNN to Faster R-CNN) prioritized training simplicity and integration. However, modern large-scale pretraining (e.g., MAE, Segment Anything) provides rich, stable visual features where the primary challenge is learning robust decision boundaries for long-tail object categories. The inherent margin-maximizing property of SVMs could offer superior generalization and robustness for the final classification layer, especially when data for specific categories is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: A decoupled training strategy, where a deep feature backbone is first trained via modern self-supervised or supervised methods, and then linear SVM classifiers are trained separately on frozen features, will yield more robust and data-efficient detection heads than end-to-end fine-tuned linear layers, particularly for rare classes.
proposals.method: We will adopt a high-capacity plain Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, pre-trained using a method like MAE. For detection, we will use a standard region proposal network (RPN) and RoIAlign. The key innovation is to freeze the backbone and extract RoI features. For each object class, we will train a linear SVM (using a library like LIBLINEAR) on these features, treating proposals as positive/negative samples. The SVMs will be trained with careful hard negative mining, reminiscent of classic R-CNN but applied to modern features. The final detection system will combine the SVM scores with bounding box regression outputs from a small, separately trained network on the same frozen features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, modern evaluation of SVMs as a post-hoc classifier for deep features, quantifying gains in robustness, out-of-distribution performance, and data efficiency. It will offer a practical, hybrid training recipe that combines the strength of modern self-supervised pretraining with the theoretical advantages of margin-based classifiers for object detection.
proposals.novelty: While SVMs were abandoned in the transition to end-to-end CNN training, their re-evaluation in the context of billion-scale pre-trained, frozen ViT backbones is novel. The proposal inverts the prevailing end-to-end paradigm by deliberately decoupling and specializing the training of the classification head.

proposals.title: Fast R-CNN for Semantic Segmentation: A Unified Framework for Detection and Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Semantic segmentation and object detection remain largely separate tasks with distinct, specialized architectures (e.g., FCNs for segmentation, R-CNN variants for detection). This fragmentation increases engineering complexity and limits knowledge transfer. A unified framework, building on the efficient Fast R-CNN paradigm, could streamline multi-task learning and leverage powerful pre-trained object detectors for segmentation, especially in data-scarce scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: The Fast R-CNN framework, which efficiently pools features from region proposals for classification and bounding-box regression, can be effectively extended to perform per-pixel semantic segmentation by adding a parallel, lightweight segmentation head, enabling a single model to excel at both tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Fast R-CNN architecture by adding a fully convolutional segmentation branch in parallel to the existing classification and bounding-box regression heads. For each Region of Interest (RoI), this branch will predict a dense pixel-wise label map. We will investigate two primary approaches: 1) a simple deconvolutional head on the RoI-pooled features, and 2) a more refined approach using a small, efficient FCN that incorporates features from earlier network stages to recover spatial detail. Training will be end-to-end on datasets like PASCAL VOC and COCO, which provide both bounding box and segmentation annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel, unified architecture capable of simultaneous object detection and semantic segmentation, reducing system complexity. It will provide a clear path to transfer strong detection priors to segmentation performance, particularly when pixel-level annotations are limited. The resulting model could serve as a strong multi-task baseline for vision systems.
proposals.novelty: While Mask R-CNN (2017) extends Faster R-CNN for instance segmentation, the proposed work uniquely explores adapting the simpler, foundational Fast R-CNN framework for semantic segmentation, focusing on architectural efficiency and the potential for a more direct knowledge transfer from pure detection models to pixel-level understanding.

proposals.title: RoI-Attention: Spatial Feature Re-weighting for Robust Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Object detection performance is often limited by irrelevant or noisy features within Region of Interest (RoI) proposals, especially for occluded or partially visible objects. While standard RoI pooling or RoIAlign extracts features, it treats all spatial locations within the RoI equally. A mechanism to adaptively weight the most discriminative features within each RoI could improve classification and localization, particularly in complex scenes. This addresses a core limitation in the post-RoI feature processing stage of two-stage detectors and RoI-based Transformer detectors.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a lightweight, learnable spatial attention module within the RoI feature extraction head will allow the model to suppress irrelevant background and foreground clutter, thereby improving feature quality for the final classification and bounding box regression tasks.
proposals.method: We propose to augment the standard RoI head in frameworks like Mask R-CNN or a ViTDet-style detector with a plug-in spatial attention module. For each RoI, after feature cropping/alignment, we will process the feature grid (e.g., 7x7xC) with a small convolutional network (e.g., two 1x1 conv layers) to produce a spatial attention map of the same spatial dimensions. This map will be normalized via sigmoid and used to perform element-wise multiplication on the original RoI features before they are fed to the final fully-connected layers. The module will be trained end-to-end with the standard detection losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a simple, effective, and interpretable module for improving RoI feature representation. We expect to demonstrate consistent accuracy gains, particularly on datasets with high occlusion or clutter (e.g., LVIS, COCO). The resulting attention maps will also offer visual insights into which parts of a proposed region the model deems most informative for recognition.
proposals.novelty: While attention is widely used in vision backbones, its application as a lightweight, post-hoc re-weighting mechanism specifically within the classical RoI head of a detector is less explored. This approach is distinct from global self-attention in DETR-style models and focuses on refining localized region features, complementing recent plain ViT backbones for detection.

proposals.title: Distilling Fast R-CNN: A Unified Framework for Efficient Object Detection via Feature and Prediction Mimicry
proposals.motivation: Large-scale, high-capacity detectors like Fast R-CNN with Vision Transformer backbones achieve state-of-the-art accuracy but are computationally prohibitive for real-time applications and edge deployment. Knowledge distillation offers a promising path to compress these models, yet existing distillation methods for detection are often fragmented, applying separate losses for features and outputs without a cohesive framework tailored for the two-stage detection paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified distillation loss, which simultaneously mimics the large teacher model's intermediate region-of-interest (RoI) features and its final classification and regression predictions, can train a significantly smaller and faster student detector (e.g., a CNN-based Fast R-CNN) to achieve accuracy much closer to the large teacher than standard supervised training alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a large, pre-trained Fast R-CNN model (e.g., with a ViT backbone from 'Exploring Plain Vision Transformer Backbones...') as the fixed teacher. A compact student model (e.g., a ResNet-based Fast R-CNN) will be trained on a target detection dataset (like COCO) using a combined loss: a standard supervised detection loss (classification + bounding box regression) and a distillation loss. The distillation loss will have two key components: 1) A feature mimicry loss on the RoI-pooled features before the box head, using L2 or a perceptual loss, and 2) A prediction mimicry loss using KL divergence on the final class probabilities and a regression loss on the teacher's refined box coordinates. We will explore adaptive weighting between these losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical and effective distillation recipe for producing efficient, high-accuracy two-stage detectors, directly enabling the deployment of powerful detection capabilities in resource-constrained environments. It will provide a systematic analysis of which components of a large detector's knowledge are most critical to transfer, advancing the understanding of distillation for structured prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While distillation for image classification is well-studied, applying it cohesively to the complex, multi-output Fast R-CNN pipeline—specifically targeting the distillation of *RoI-aligned features* alongside task-specific heads—is a non-trivial extension. This approach moves beyond simply distilling backbone features or final scores, explicitly addressing the unique architecture of region-based detectors.

proposals.title: Non-Local Networks for Dense Prediction: A Unified Study on Instance Segmentation and Depth Estimation
proposals.motivation: Non-local blocks, introduced by Wang et al. (2018), effectively capture long-range dependencies in video and image classification. However, their systematic application to a broader set of dense prediction tasks remains underexplored. Given the success of attention mechanisms in vision transformers and the need for global context in tasks like instance segmentation and monocular depth estimation, a rigorous evaluation of non-local operations in these settings is timely and could lead to more powerful, unified architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating non-local blocks into modern convolutional or hybrid backbones for dense prediction will consistently improve performance on tasks requiring fine-grained localization and global scene understanding, by effectively modeling long-range pixel relationships without a full transformer-based redesign.
proposals.method: We will integrate non-local blocks into established frameworks for instance segmentation (Mask R-CNN) and depth estimation (Dense Prediction Transformers). For a controlled study, we will use plain ViT and ConvNeXt backbones, inserting non-local blocks at specific stages. We will benchmark on COCO (instance segmentation) and NYU Depth V2, using a consistent training recipe (supervised learning with strong augmentation). The core experiment will compare baseline performance against architectures augmented with different placements (early vs. late) and formulations (simplified, Gaussian) of non-local blocks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, empirical benchmark for the utility of non-local blocks across key dense prediction tasks, moving beyond initial demonstrations in video. It will deliver practical architectural guidelines for integrating long-range modeling into detection and segmentation pipelines. The findings could inform the design of more efficient hybrid models that combine the inductive biases of CNNs with the global reasoning of attention.
proposals.novelty: While non-local blocks are known, their systematic application and direct comparison against emerging transformer-based methods for instance-level segmentation and depth estimation is novel. The study focuses on a pragmatic integration into high-performance, supervised detection frameworks, a natural extension of Dr. Girshick's expertise, rather than proposing a wholly new attention mechanism.

proposals.title: Supervised Momentum Contrast: Improving Representation Consistency and Transferability with a Dynamic Labeled Dictionary
proposals.motivation: The Momentum Contrast (MoCo) framework has proven highly effective for self-supervised representation learning by maintaining a large, consistent dictionary of negative samples. However, its application has been largely confined to the unsupervised domain. In supervised learning, especially for detection and segmentation, models can overfit to dataset-specific biases and lack the robust, transferable features learned via contrastive objectives. This work investigates whether the core MoCo mechanics—a momentum-updated encoder and a large queue-based dictionary—can be repurposed to improve supervised representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a momentum dictionary populated with labeled instance features into a standard supervised training pipeline will enforce more consistent and transferable representations by providing a stable, large set of contrasting class prototypes, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks like object detection and segmentation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MoCo-v2/3 framework for supervised learning. For each labeled image batch, features will be extracted via a query encoder (e.g., a ViT or ResNet). A momentum encoder will generate corresponding keys for the same images. The dictionary will be a queue of key features from previous batches, each associated with its ground-truth class label. The contrastive loss will be a supervised variant of InfoNCE, where the positive pair is the query and its matching momentum key, and negatives are drawn from the dictionary, potentially reweighted by label similarity. We will integrate this loss with standard cross-entropy classification loss and evaluate on ImageNet pre-training followed by transfer to COCO detection and LVIS segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for injecting contrastive learning principles directly into supervised training, potentially yielding more robust and transferable backbones for vision tasks. It will expand the utility of the momentum dictionary concept beyond self-supervision and offer new insights into representation consistency. The resulting models could improve the performance and data efficiency of supervised pre-training for detection and segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While MoCo is established for self-supervised learning, applying its momentum dictionary mechanism explicitly to supervised learning with labeled data is a novel direction. The key innovation is maintaining a dynamic dictionary of *labeled* features to provide a consistent set of supervised contrastive targets during training, which is distinct from standard supervised contrastive losses that operate only within a batch.

proposals.title: Learning to Compose: Non-Additive Branch Fusion for Reconstruction from Extremely Sparse Context
proposals.motivation: Masked image modeling (MIM) has proven powerful for self-supervised representation learning, but high masking ratios (e.g., 75% in MAE) are standard, potentially limiting the model's ability to perform fine-grained reasoning from dense, visible cues. Conversely, training with very low masking ratios (<5%) using standard additive fusion of encoder branches may lead to trivial solutions and redundant representations, failing to leverage the unique, complementary information in each unmasked token's local context. This work investigates whether imposing a non-trivial compositional challenge during low-signal reconstruction can force the emergence of more diverse and powerful visual features.
proposals.hypothesis: Forcing a model to reconstruct masked image content from an extremely sparse set of visible tokens (<5%) using a non-additive, context-sensitive fusion of parallel processing branches will yield more specialized, complementary feature representations and superior reasoning abilities, leading to stronger performance on downstream dense prediction tasks like detection and segmentation.
proposals.method: We will design a vision transformer encoder where a small set of unmasked tokens are processed in parallel by multiple, independent branch networks (e.g., small MLPs or lightweight ViT blocks). Critically, their outputs will be fused via a learned, non-additive function, such as a cross-branch attention module or a dynamic gating network conditioned on a global context vector. This fusion mechanism must integrate the specialized signals to predict masked tokens via a lightweight decoder. We will train this system end-to-end using a reconstruction loss (e.g., MSE or perceptual loss) on large-scale image datasets like ImageNet-1K, systematically evaluating the impact of the fusion mechanism versus simple averaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural framework for self-supervised learning under extreme sparsity, demonstrating the value of learned compositional fusion over simple aggregation. It will deliver new insights into how to encourage representational diversity and complex reasoning in visual backbones, with empirical validation on transfer learning benchmarks for object detection (COCO) and segmentation (ADE20K).
proposals.novelty: While prior work like MAE explores high masking ratios and simple averaging, and models like Mixture-of-Experts use gating for routing, this proposal uniquely combines an extremely low masking ratio with an architectural mandate for non-additive branch fusion, creating a novel and constrained compositional learning problem for visual representation learning.

proposals.title: SAM-FPN: Leveraging Segment Anything's Hierarchical Features for Multi-Scale Object Detection
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) has demonstrated remarkable zero-shot segmentation capability, but its rich, multi-scale feature representations remain underexplored for object-level recognition tasks. Concurrently, object detection models, especially those built on plain Vision Transformers (ViTs), often rely on handcrafted feature pyramid networks (FPNs) that are not co-optimized with a foundational vision model's feature hierarchy. This work investigates whether the intrinsic multi-scale features from a billion-scale segmentation model can provide a superior, semantically rich foundation for multi-scale object detection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hierarchical feature maps from a frozen SAM image encoder, when integrated via a lightweight, adaptive feature pyramid module, will provide a more robust and semantically grounded multi-scale representation for object detection than standard, task-specifically trained FPNs built on supervised or self-supervised backbones.
proposals.method: We will extract multi-scale feature maps from the frozen SAM ViT-H image encoder. A lightweight, parameter-efficient Adaptive Feature Refinement Module (AFRM) will be designed to align and fuse these features into a SAM Feature Pyramid Network (SAM-FPN). This pyramid will serve as the neck for a standard detection head (e.g., Mask R-CNN). The primary experiments will involve fine-tuning only the AFRM and the detection head on COCO, comparing against detection systems built on MAE-pretrained or supervised ViTs with standard FPNs. We will analyze the transfer performance on long-tail and cross-domain datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic evaluation of SAM's features for dense prediction beyond segmentation, establishing a new strong baseline for transfer learning in detection. It will contribute a novel, model-agnostic method for building feature pyramids from foundational vision models, potentially reducing the need for extensive detection-specific pretraining.
proposals.novelty: While FPNs are standard, constructing them from the frozen features of a massive, promptable segmentation model (SAM) for detection is novel. The key insight is to treat SAM as a foundational feature extractor whose hierarchical representations are inherently tuned for spatial and semantic reasoning at multiple scales.

ideas.title: Multi-Resolution Staged Pre-training with Decoupled Reconstruction and Contrastive Objectives
ideas.description: This system decouples the reconstruction and contrastive learning objectives into a two-stage process to avoid optimization conflict. First, a deep encoder-decoder is trained with a strong masked autoencoding objective to build a comprehensive, multi-scale feature pyramid. Second, a small, trainable projection head is attached to multiple layers of the now-frozen encoder and trained with a contrastive loss on differently augmented views of the data, learning invariance at appropriate feature resolutions. A lightweight semantic alignment module ensures the contrastive features remain grounded in the semantically rich features from the reconstruction stage, producing a unified model robust for both pixel-level and semantic tasks.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Relational Reconstruction from Jointly Masked Multiview Inputs
ideas.description: This system receives multiple images (e.g., different views of an object, or a scene at different times) which are jointly and randomly masked. The model's task is not to reconstruct any single original image, but to predict a relational property defined across the full, unmasked set, such as the viewpoint transformation, the semantic difference, or a fused summary representation. This forces the model to integrate information from all partial views to solve the task, learning robust cross-image representations. The approach is evaluated on downstream tasks requiring reasoning across multiple images, such as 3D understanding, visual correspondence, and state-change prediction.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Granularity Masked Modeling for Spatiotemporal and Cross-Modal Structure Learning
ideas.description: This approach extends the MAE framework by moving beyond simple patch-based reconstruction to explicitly model the hierarchical structure of video and multi-modal data. The system employs strategically designed masking strategies at different levels of abstraction—such as masking object regions across frames, temporal segments of audio, or an entire modality—and couples this with a multi-task reconstruction objective. The model must predict raw pixels or waveforms, intermediate features like motion or mel-spectrograms, and cross-modal correspondences, ensuring the learned representations capture essential spatiotemporal dynamics and semantic alignment between modalities. This results in more robust, generalizable features for downstream tasks like action recognition, audio-visual scene understanding, and video question answering.

ideas.title: Non-additive Branch Fusion for Low-Masking Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research direction investigates training a model with an extremely low masking ratio (e.g., <5%) but with a novel architectural constraint: the parallel transformation branches that process the unmasked tokens cannot be combined via simple addition or averaging. Instead, the model must learn a context-sensitive, non-additive function (e.g., gating, attention, or a learned compositional network) to fuse these branch outputs into a coherent prediction for the masked regions. This forces each branch to develop highly specialized, complementary representations from the sparse visible context, and the fusion mechanism must solve the non-trivial puzzle of integrating these signals for accurate reconstruction. The core hypothesis is that this dual challenge—learning from minimal signal and learning to combine specialized features in complex ways—will lead to models with stronger representational diversity and superior reasoning for reconstruction and downstream understanding tasks.

ideas.title: Predicting Chromosomal Proximity from Genomic Sequence Patches
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational genomics, where models predict the 3D spatial proximity of DNA sequence patches (e.g., enhancer-promoter contacts) from linear sequence data, using labels from experimental Hi-C maps. The analogous solution is a binary classification task on paired sequence segments. Adapted to computer vision, the research idea is to train a model on a 'next-patch prediction' task for images. The model receives two non-adjacent image patches and must predict if they were originally spatially proximate in the scene. This pre-training objective, inspired by genomic interaction prediction, encourages the learning of global structural and relational semantics between image regions, moving beyond local feature detection.

ideas.title: Adversarial Feature Learning with Minimal Masking
ideas.description: This research investigates whether very low masking ratios (e.g., <10%) can be effective when paired with a dynamic adversarial objective rather than a standard reconstruction loss. The core system introduces a lightweight critic network trained to distinguish between reconstructions from a model that merely copies visible patches and one that performs genuine inference. The primary model is then trained to minimize reconstruction error while simultaneously maximizing the critic's error, forcing it to develop robust, high-level representations from minimal context. This approach ensures the learning signal remains strong despite low masking, potentially leading to more data-efficient and feature-rich pre-training.

ideas.title: Relational Mask Prediction: Learning to Infer Patch Interactions from Reconstruction Discrepancy
ideas.description: This research trains a model to predict a relational masking pattern, rather than a simple binary mask, based on the reconstruction error of a masked autoencoder. The core task is to predict, for a given pair of image patches, whether masking one makes the other harder to reconstruct, directly translating the 'next sentence prediction' objective to visual relational reasoning. This forces the model to learn a graph of patch interdependencies, where edge weights represent predictive utility or co-dependency. The inverse task of generating masks from error thus becomes a probe for the model's internalized understanding of scene composition and object-part relationships.

ideas.title: Dynamic-Cardinality Multi-Image Masked Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research explores a vision transformer architecture for the task of reconstructing a target image from the patched content of multiple, simultaneously masked source images. The key innovation is that the network's cardinality—the number of parallel pathways or expert groups within a layer—varies dynamically across different processing stages. Early layers with high cardinality can separately process disparate visual concepts from the different source images, while later layers with lower cardinality force the integration of these concepts into a coherent single-image reconstruction. This dynamic structure allows the model to learn an optimal strategy for fusing multi-image context, balancing specialized feature extraction with holistic synthesis.

ideas.title: Multi-Level Feature Reconstruction with Adversarial Manifold Consistency
ideas.description: This approach reconstructs hierarchical feature targets from a frozen pre-trained model rather than raw pixels, avoiding low-level signal noise. To prevent degenerate solutions, it employs a multi-level consistency loss that aligns reconstructions across several semantic layers of the teacher network. Simultaneously, an adversarial critic regularizes the process by ensuring features decode back to a consistent and plausible data manifold, such as a pixel space or a simple attribute space. This results in learned representations that are both semantically rich and grounded, improving downstream task transfer and robustness.

ideas.title: Multi-Image Cross-Modal Diffusion Denoising Pre-training
ideas.description: This approach pre-trains a vision model by presenting it with multiple masked source images and a heavily noised target image. The model's objective is to iteratively denoise the target image, but it must do so by reasoning across the visible patches from all source images to infer the missing or corrupted content. This combines the structured, step-wise generative process of diffusion with the cross-contextual understanding required for multi-image reconstruction. The resulting model learns a powerful, scene-aware latent space capable of coherently synthesizing novel content from disparate visual cues.

ideas.title: Contiguous Masking via Ecological Gap Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of independent random patch masking in self-supervised visual pre-training by drawing an analogy from Landscape Ecology. In that field, canopy gaps are modeled as contiguous areas from discrete events, not independent pixel losses. The analogous solution uses spatial stochastic models to generate realistic, autocorrelated gap structures. Adapted to machine learning, this means generating masks by simulating contiguous 'occlusion events' (e.g., using random shapes, cluster growth, or diffusion processes) on the input. This structured, contiguous masking strategy better approximates real-world occlusions and should compel the model to develop stronger reasoning about object parts and holistic context, rather than relying on scattered, independent evidence.

ideas.title: Theoretical Framework for Low-Masking, High-Cardinality Pre-training
ideas.description: This research fuses the investigation of low masking ratios with the theoretical analysis of cardinality benefits to develop a novel framework for self-supervised learning. It posits that very low masking ratios, when paired with objectives that maximize the cardinality (e.g., diversity of predicted targets or feature transformations), can yield superior data efficiency and representation quality by preserving more of the original data's statistical structure. The core synergy is a theoretical model explaining how low masking increases the effective cardinality and informativeness of each training example, reducing the need for destructive corruption while still providing a complex prediction task. This leads to a new class of pre-training objectives that are theoretically grounded in optimizing the trade-off between data preservation and prediction task complexity.

ideas.title: Phased Mask Processing for Stable Symmetric Representation Learning
ideas.description: This research explores a symmetric encoder-only architecture that processes both clean and masked tokens without degrading representation quality. It achieves this through a strict, phased separation of training: the model alternates between processing only clean data to build robust features and processing masked data to learn reconstruction. A trainable, input-dependent gating network dynamically blends the outputs of these two operational modes for the final prediction. This design maintains the efficiency and symmetry goal of the original idea while directly preventing the corruption of the core representation learning mechanism.

ideas.title: Reconstructing Semantic Primitives Instead of Sensory Data
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Cognitive Psychology and Psycholinguistics, where humans are found to store the semantic 'gist' (propositional representations) rather than exact sensory details of language. The analogous solution is encoding experiences as abstract structures of concepts and relationships. Adapted to machine learning, this suggests relaxing the assumption of reconstructing raw pixels by instead reconstructing high-level semantic primitives. The research idea is to train a model (e.g., an autoencoder) to reconstruct a discrete set of learned semantic concepts or feature clusters extracted by a frozen pretrained network, using a loss on the semantic representation fidelity. This prioritizes capturing essential meaning in the latent space, potentially improving robustness, compression, and generalization by discarding irrelevant perceptual noise.

ideas.title: Explore SAM for 3D scene understanding
ideas.description: Adapt the promptable segmentation framework to 3D data like point clouds or medical volumes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Segment Anything

ideas.title: Immunological Co-Training for Robust Representation Learning
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational immunology. The analogous problem is how biological immune systems achieve robust pathogen recognition through the complementary, co-trained innate and adaptive subsystems. The analogous solution involves models where a general, context-reconstructing innate module informs a specific, contrastive adaptive module. Adapted to machine learning, this becomes a method called 'Immunological Co-Training for Robust Representation Learning.' The architecture features two pathways: a reconstruction pathway that performs MAE-style masked input prediction to learn general features, and a parallel discrimination pathway that applies a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on the latent representations generated by the reconstruction pathway. This dual objective ensures the model learns not only to reconstruct data but also to develop a latent space where semantically similar instances are clustered effectively, leading to more transferable and robust representations for downstream tasks.

ideas.title: Pre-training Vision Transformers via Thermodynamic Denoising of Masked States
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from statistical physics, where a system relaxes to equilibrium through probabilistic, entropy-governed steps, this idea adapts that process for vision transformer pre-training. The analogous solution from thermodynamics involves modeling the irreversible path from a perturbed state back to equilibrium. Adapted to machine learning, we replace masked patch reconstruction with a training objective where the model must perform iterative denoising on heavily noised masked patches. The model learns to predict the progression of a diffusion process for each token, moving from a noisy 'non-equilibrium' state towards the clean data distribution, thereby learning a more robust and generative representation of visual data during pre-training.

ideas.title: Semantic Masking for Branch-Specialized Input Representation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel pre-training architecture where a model's parallel processing branches receive different, semantically structured views of the input. Instead of random masking, each branch is trained with a distinct, consistent masking strategy (e.g., one branch receives only noun phrases, another only verbs and relations, a third only global context) to force specialization. This creates a synergistic effect: the structured masking provides a clear learning signal for each branch to develop unique expertise, while the variable input to branches ensures the model learns to fuse these specialized representations effectively. The result is a single model that internally decomposes and reasons about data along fundamental semantic axes, improving both data efficiency and downstream task performance on tasks requiring compositional understanding.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Multi-Branch Networks with Adaptive MAE-Supervised Hybrid Objectives
ideas.description: This research proposes a neural architecture where branches within a cardinality group (e.g., in a ResNeXt or multi-expert system) are not constrained to identical topologies or hyperparameters. Each heterogeneous branch is paired with a customized training objective that adaptively combines Masked Autoencoder (MAE) self-supervision with traditional supervised or semi-supervised loss components. This allows one branch to focus on reconstructing heavily masked inputs for robust feature learning, while another concurrently processes less corrupted data with a strong supervised signal, enabling specialized, complementary representations within a unified model. The synergy creates a form of 'internal curriculum learning,' where the architecture and its training objectives co-adapt to efficiently leverage both labeled and unlabeled data across diverse subtasks.

ideas.title: Adaptive Masking Curriculum via Self-Supervised Learning Progress Signals
ideas.description: This approach introduces a meta-learning framework where the masking strategy is not pre-defined but is dynamically adapted during training. The system employs a set of online performance metrics, derived from the model's own training behavior, to assess the current learning difficulty. Based on these signals, a lightweight controller network or policy updates the masking parameters in real-time, aiming to keep the model in a state of high learning efficiency. This creates a closed-loop training system that tailors the masking curriculum to the specific model and dataset, optimizing the pre-training process for robustness and final task performance.

ideas.title: Multi-Source Signal Fusion via Joint Masking and Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'jointly mask and reconstruct multiple images' concept by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic imaging. In that field, a joint inversion process fuses incomplete signals from multiple sources to reconstruct a single model of the subsurface. Adapted here, the research involves training a neural network model where the input consists of patches sampled from several different images, each heavily masked in distinct, non-overlapping patterns. The model's dual task is to reason across these multiple fragmented contexts—acting as complementary 'sources' of visual information—to reconstruct one coherent target image. This forces the model to develop robust fusion and cross-contextual reasoning abilities, scaling learning by leveraging the shared structure across the masked inputs to solve a more constrained and informationally sparse reconstruction puzzle.

ideas.title: Semantically-Anchored Extreme Masking for MAE: Preventing Degenerate Hallucinations
ideas.description: This research pushes MAE masking ratios beyond 90% but directly combats the tendency to produce generic, ungrounded outputs by enforcing semantic fidelity. The system incorporates a dual-objective loss function that combines pixel-level reconstruction with a semantic consistency loss derived from a frozen foundation model's embedding space. This forces the model to use the extremely sparse visible patches not just for low-level texture synthesis, but to infer and preserve the high-level semantic content of the original image. The approach rigorously tests the limits of reconstruction while ensuring the task remains meaningful and the model's outputs are verifiably anchored to the specific input.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal MAE via Geophysical Tomography Principles
ideas.description: This research expands the MAE framework to multi-modal data (e.g., video+audio+text) by drawing an analogy from geophysical tomography. In tomography, a complete 3D model is reconstructed from sparse, multi-angle sensor data using physical constraints. Analogously, we propose a Multi-Modal MAE that masks large random portions of all input streams (visual patches, audio frames, text tokens) simultaneously. The reconstruction objective forces the model to learn a unified latent representation by inferring missing content in one modality not only from its own sparse signals but also from the correlated, unmasked context provided by the other modalities. This cross-modal inference mimics the tomographic principle, using multi-perspective 'sensors' (modalities) to reconstruct a complete multi-modal scene representation, enabling powerful self-supervised pre-training for complex, real-world tasks.

ideas.title: Symmetric Mask-Processing Encoder Inspired by Bidirectional Cellular Communication
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that encoder and decoder must be asymmetric in masked modeling tasks, specifically that the encoder should only process unmasked tokens. Drawing an analogy from immunology, where antigen-presenting cells ingest, process, and present contextualized fragments of both self and foreign material, we propose a symmetric architecture. In this adapted model, the encoder ingests the full sequence containing both visible and mask tokens. Through its layered processing, it doesn't just create representations for visible tokens; it actively generates contextualized predictions for the mask tokens as well, effectively 'presenting' a completed representation. This could allow the training objective (e.g., reconstruction) to be applied directly to the encoder's output, merging the representation learning and reconstruction functions into a single, symmetric network and questioning the necessity of a dedicated decoder.

ideas.title: Context-Routed Mixture of Sparse Experts for Patch Reconstruction
ideas.description: This system overcomes the rigidity of standard sparse coding for reconstruction by employing a mixture-of-experts framework where each 'expert' is a specialized sparse dictionary tailored to a different type of visual pattern. A lightweight routing network, conditioned on the unmasked context surrounding the target patch, dynamically assigns weights to these experts for each reconstruction task. This allows the model to adaptively combine relevant priors—such as textural, structural, or smooth—based on the local image content. The result is a more flexible and semantically accurate reconstruction process that maintains the benefits of sparsity while being robust to the diversity of natural image patches.

ideas.title: Learnable Cardinality Policy Networks via Differentiable Architecture Search
ideas.description: This research proposes to learn how cardinality should vary across network depth, rather than searching it as a static hyperparameter. A small controller network observes feature statistics or layer indices and outputs a cardinality value for each block, which is then used to instantiate the ResNeXt-style grouped convolutions. The controller's parameters are trained jointly with the main network using gradient-based techniques or efficient reinforcement learning, enforcing a resource budget to prevent trivial solutions. This results in an architecture that automatically discovers and adapts its width-complexity profile for a given task and compute constraint, leading to more robust and transferable design principles.

ideas.title: Synergy-Maximizing Combination of Parallel Branches via Differentiable Information Bottleneck
ideas.description: This research proposes a principled framework for combining parallel transformation branches by explicitly optimizing for synergistic information, rather than exploring non-additive functions heuristically. The core method involves a trainable combination module that is regularized by a differentiable Partial Information Decomposition (PID) loss, encouraging the combined representation to capture information that is jointly provided by all branches but not present in any subset. This approach ensures the combination mechanism learns to create genuinely emergent features, improving model robustness and generalization by design. The system is evaluated by measuring synergy gains on out-of-distribution tasks and adversarial robustness benchmarks, providing both a strong performance baseline and a clear theoretical interpretation for the learned combination strategy.

ideas.title: Sparse Tomographic Reconstruction for MAE with Extreme Masking
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to sparse-view tomographic reconstruction from medical imaging to the problem of Masked Autoencoding (MAE) with masking ratios exceeding 90%. In computed tomography, high-quality 3D images are routinely reconstructed from a very small number of 2D projections—an analogous problem of recovering a whole from a tiny, information-sparse subset. The adapted solution is to redesign the MAE training objective and decoder architecture to explicitly perform iterative, prior-constrained reconstruction. The model would learn not just to predict pixels, but to solve an inverse problem by imposing learned structural and semantic priors at each iteration in a latent space, thereby testing the fundamental limits of what global context and learned priors can recover from near-total local information loss.

ideas.title: Multi-Spectral Ensemble for Robust Classification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that parallel branches in a neural architecture must operate on identical input representations. Drawing an analogy from remote sensing, where classifiers use different spectral bands of the same scene to identify distinct features, we propose a 'Multi-Spectral Ensemble' architecture. Each parallel branch receives a uniquely transformed or subsetted version of the input (e.g., frequency-domain, spatially cropped, or semantically filtered representations). These branches become specialists for features detectable in their assigned 'input spectrum'. The solutions from all branches are then fused, potentially using an adaptive gating mechanism, to build a final output that is robust and leverages complementary information pathways, much like fusing multispectral satellite imagery yields a more accurate land cover map than any single band.

ideas.title: Learned Dynamic Input Routing for Parallel Branches
ideas.description: This approach replaces static input assignment with a trainable routing module that dynamically allocates input information to parallel processing branches. The router analyzes the input and computes a soft, differentiable distribution of feature relevance across branches, enabling context-dependent specialization. This allows the network to learn which branches should focus on which input aspects, improving representational efficiency and robustness. The entire system, including the router and branches, is optimized jointly via gradient descent.

ideas.title: Topology-Aware Branch Alignment with Dynamic Divergence Control
ideas.description: This system enables branches within a cardinality group to have different structures while maintaining stable and coherent learning. It enforces a shared semantic representation space across all branches via a differentiable alignment objective, ensuring their outputs remain comparable. A lightweight meta-controller dynamically adjusts the allowable degree of architectural divergence based on real-time measurements of gradient alignment and feature similarity. This approach preserves the original idea's flexibility for specialized processing but guarantees training stability and effective feature aggregation.

ideas.title: Progressive Masking via Fitts's Law and Motor Skill Acquisition
ideas.description: This idea adapts curriculum learning strategies from motor skill acquisition and human-computer interaction principles to the problem of training with masking. The analogous field solves the problem of learning complex physical skills through progressive overload and part-task training, often guided by models like Fitts's Law which quantifies task difficulty through parameters like target size and distance. The adapted solution is to design a multi-dimensional masking curriculum where difficulty is progressively increased not just by the percentage of tokens masked, but by systematically varying the spatial and structural parameters of the masks—such as shrinking the average size of unmasked context windows, increasing the average distance between masked segments, and progressing from random masking to structured, grammatically challenging masking patterns. This aims to build a more robust and generalizable language model by simulating a structured 'skill acquisition' regimen for its predictive capabilities.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Cardinality Management via Pareto-Optimal Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research develops a system that dynamically manages model cardinality (e.g., number of experts, attention heads, or latent categories) as a constrained resource during training and inference. Instead of seeking a single static optimum, it continuously evaluates a Pareto frontier balancing task performance, computational budget, and model stability. The system employs lightweight meta-learned controllers that adjust cardinality allocations across model components in response to gradient signals, data difficulty, and hardware constraints, ensuring efficient specialization. This results in models that can autonomously adapt their structural complexity to the demands of the specific task and operational environment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Branch-Specific Transformations via Meta-Learning
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to investigate the integration of cardinality with advanced transformations by drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology. In Evo-Devo, diverse morphological traits arise from the contextual application of a shared genetic toolkit to different developmental branches. Adapting this solution, the research will develop a meta-learning framework where a core neural module—analogous to a developmental gene regulatory network—generates the parameters for complex, branch-specific transformations (e.g., attention mechanisms, invertible flows, or graph convolutions) conditioned on the cardinality of the data substructure and its latent state. This approach aims to create more powerful, efficient, and generalizable multi-branch architectures where transformation complexity is dynamically specialized per branch without sacrificing parameter efficiency or the ability to share learned representational primitives across the system.

ideas.title: Cardinality-Branched Transformers for Ultra-High Masking Ratio Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel masked autoencoder (MAE) architecture designed to operate at extreme masking ratios (e.g., 95-99%). The core innovation is replacing standard transformer blocks with a cardinality-branched structure, where each parallel branch applies a distinct, potentially learned, non-linear transformation (e.g., spectral, wavelet, or sparse coding) to the tiny subset of visible tokens. The model must then fuse these diversely transformed, minimal representations to perform the reconstruction, testing the limits of what constitutes a sufficient 'seed' for generation. This forces the system to develop highly efficient and abstract internal representations, potentially revealing fundamental data invariants and new forms of compression.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Branch Networks with Dynamic Compositional Gating
ideas.description: This research direction proposes designing neural building blocks composed of parallel, architecturally heterogeneous branches—each with distinct operations, depths, or receptive fields—whose outputs are combined not by simple addition or concatenation, but by a dynamic, input-dependent compositional gate. This gate learns to synthesize branch outputs through non-additive operations like modulated fusion, element-wise product, or soft selection, creating a meta-operation tailored to the interaction of the specific heterogeneous features. Consequently, the network gains the ability to adaptively compose complex transformations from a set of specialized, diverse components, leading to more expressive and computationally efficient blocks than homogeneous multi-branch designs with fixed aggregation.

ideas.title: Causal Scaling Laws: Disentangling and Isolating the Contributions of Model, Data, and Compute in Masked Autoencoder Performance
ideas.description: This research moves beyond observational scaling plots to establish causal scaling laws for Masked Autoencoders (MAEs). Through a massively parallelized, controlled training campaign across a grid of model sizes, dataset sizes, and compute budgets, it performs a full factorial experiment to isolate the individual and interactive effects of each scaling dimension. The analysis employs statistical modeling and ablation techniques to distinguish fundamental architectural scaling properties from optimization artifacts or dataset confounders. The outcome is a predictive, mechanistic framework that specifies how to optimally allocate resources across model parameters, data, and training compute for MAE-based systems.

ideas.title: Information-Preserving Cardinality Branches with Consistency Constraints
ideas.description: This approach investigates using complex, learned transformations within a multi-branch cardinality framework, but crucially imposes constraints to prevent representational distortion. Each branch learns a distinct nonlinear mapping, but all mappings are regularized by a shared autoencoding objective to preserve input information. Simultaneously, a consistency loss minimizes divergence between the transformed representations from different branches, ensuring they capture complementary yet semantically coherent views of the data. This results in a robust architecture where advanced transformations enhance diversity without compromising the integrity and comparability of the branched representations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Vascular Branching for Dynamic Network Cardinality
ideas.description: This research seed proposes studying architectures where the cardinality (e.g., as in ResNeXt groups) varies dynamically across network stages. Drawing an analogy from physiology, specifically vascular system development where branching cardinality adapts to local metabolic demand via feedback signals like VEGF, we adapt this solution to deep learning. The adapted idea is to design a neural network where a lightweight, trainable controller module monitors local feature complexity metrics (akin to metabolic demand) and dynamically adjusts the number of parallel transformation paths (cardinality) in each layer or block. This creates a non-uniform, demand-driven architecture that can expand capacity where needed for complex representations and prune it for efficiency in simpler regions, optimizing the overall parameter and compute budget.

ideas.title: Sparse Sampling for Signal Reconstruction in Masked Language Modeling
ideas.description: This idea inverts the high masking ratio requirement in masked language modeling by drawing an analogy from compressed sensing in signal processing. In that field, perfect reconstruction of a sparse signal is possible from very few, incoherent samples by using an L1-norm minimization objective that promotes sparsity. Adapted to MLM, the research would investigate whether applying a very low masking ratio (1-5%) with strategically random, non-adjacent patterns (incoherent 'sampling' of the token sequence) can be highly effective when the model's training objective is augmented to not just predict the masked tokens, but to do so in a way that encourages the learned representations to be sparse in a transformed domain. The solution from the analogous field suggests the critical innovation is not the amount of masking, but changing the reconstruction objective to exploit an assumed sparsity structure in language, potentially leading to more data-efficient pre-training.
